meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) oscillations, waves, and sound items. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e Ch. 16-17 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). Ch. 17 had many five-option, 'select two', figure-based, and free-response items, which were skipped; two Ch. 17 items were reformatted from a 4-row answer table into A-D choices without changing the wording.

questions.stem: Which of the following represents the distance (how much ground the particle covers) moved by a particle in simple harmonic motion in one time period? (A represents the amplitude of the oscillation.)
questions.explanation: In one full period the particle travels from its position out to one extreme, back through equilibrium to the other extreme, and back again, covering a total path length of A + A + A + A = 4A. (Its displacement over one period is zero, but the distance traveled is 4A.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim (units 'cm' in the original generalized to amplitude A). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: In simple harmonic motion, the amplitude A is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the maximum distance from equilibrium to one extreme

choices.label: B
choices.text: the full distance between the two extremes

choices.label: C
choices.text: the distance covered in one full period

hints.rationale: Amplitude is the maximum displacement from equilibrium to an extreme.

hints.prompt: In HALF a period, the particle travels from one extreme all the way to the other extreme. What distance is that (in terms of A)?
hints.rationale: From one extreme to the other is two amplitudes, 2A (a full period is two such trips).

hints.prompt: The DISTANCE (path length) over a full period is nonzero (0 is the displacement) and more than a single one-way trip. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 0 is the net displacement (not the distance) and A is too small for a full cycle, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: In the formula F = -kx, what does the minus sign indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It indicates that the restoring force is in the direction of the displacement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It indicates that the restoring force is in the direction opposite the displacement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It indicates that mechanical energy in the system decreases when a system undergoes oscillation.

questions.explanation: Hooke's law restoring force always points back toward equilibrium, i.e., opposite to the displacement x. The negative sign encodes that opposition.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Hooke's law.

hints.prompt: A 'restoring force' in an oscillator always points:

choices.label: C
choices.text: in the direction of increasing displacement

hints.rationale: A restoring force points back toward equilibrium.

hints.prompt: Pointing 'back toward equilibrium' means the force is directed ____ the displacement x from equilibrium.
hints.rationale: Toward equilibrium is opposite to the displacement, which the minus sign encodes.

hints.prompt: The minus sign in F = −kx describes the force's DIRECTION, not energy loss. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'it indicates mechanical energy decreases during oscillation' and 'None of the above'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'restoring force is in the direction of the displacement' and 'restoring force is in the direction opposite the displacement'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'restoring force is in the direction of the displacement' and 'None of the above'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'it indicates mechanical energy decreases during oscillation' and 'restoring force is in the direction opposite the displacement'

hints.rationale: The sign encodes direction, not energy loss, so the 'energy decreases' and 'none of the above' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: The splashing of a liquid resembles an oscillation. The restoring force in this scenario will be due to which of the following?
questions.explanation: When liquid is displaced from its level surface, gravity pulls the raised liquid back down, acting as the restoring force that drives the sloshing oscillation. (Energy is not a force.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (gravity restores a displaced liquid surface).

hints.prompt: The RESTORING agent of an oscillation must be a force (not an energy). Which of these is an actual force?
hints.rationale: Gravity is a force; the others are forms of energy, not forces.

hints.prompt: Some choices name 'potential energy' or 'kinetic energy' as the restoring agent. Why can't an ENERGY be the restoring force?

choices.label: A
choices.text: energy is a scalar quantity, not a force, so it cannot BE the restoring force

choices.label: B
choices.text: energy is actually a kind of force

choices.label: C
choices.text: because energy always points toward equilibrium

choices.label: D
choices.text: it can—energy is the restoring force

hints.rationale: A restoring force must be a force; energy is not a force, so energy-named options are ruled out.

hints.prompt: A restoring FORCE cannot be a form of energy. Which pair of energy options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Potential and kinetic energy are energies, not forces, so both are eliminated as the restoring force.

questions.stem: A particle of mass 100 g undergoes simple harmonic motion. The restoring force is provided by a spring with a spring constant of 40 N·m⁻¹. What is the period of oscillation?
questions.explanation: For a mass-spring oscillator, T = 2π√(m/k) = 2π√(0.100 kg / 40 N·m⁻¹) = 2π√(0.0025) = 2π(0.05) = 0.1π s (≈ 0.31 s).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (T = 2π√(m/k)).

hints.prompt: The period of a mass–spring oscillator is:
hints.rationale: T = 2π√(m/k) for a mass on a spring.

hints.prompt: Convert to SI and set up the ratio: m = 100 g = 0.100 kg, k = 40 N/m. What is m/k inside the square root?
The period T = 2π√(m/k) for this small mass on a fairly stiff spring comes out well under 1π s—nowhere near 1π or 10π s. Which pair of options can be eliminated as too large?
hints.rationale: The period comes out to only a fraction of π (less than 1π s), so the values 10π s and 1π s are far too large and eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following gives the correct relation between the acceleration due to gravity g and the period T of a simple pendulum of length L?
questions.explanation: The simple pendulum period is T = 2π√(L/g). Squaring gives T² = 4π²L/g, and solving for g yields g = 4π²L/T².
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by rearranging T = 2π√(L/g).

hints.prompt: The period of a simple pendulum of length L is:
hints.rationale: A simple pendulum has T = 2π√(L/g).

hints.prompt: Square both sides of T = 2π√(L/g). What do you get?
hints.rationale: Squaring gives T² = 4π² L/g.

hints.prompt: Solving T² = 4π²L/g for g puts T² (not T) in the denominator. Which pair of options can be eliminated because they have only T (first power) in the denominator?
hints.rationale: g must have T² in the denominator, so both options with only T are eliminated.

questions.stem: In the equation x = A sin(ωt), what values can the position x take?
questions.explanation: The sine function ranges from -1 to +1, so x = A sin(ωt) ranges from -A to +A. A is the amplitude, the maximum displacement from equilibrium.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (range of A sin is -A to +A).

hints.prompt: The sine function, sin(θ), always takes values in the range:
hints.rationale: Sine oscillates between −1 and +1.

hints.prompt: In x = A sin(ωt), what does the constant A represent physically?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the amplitude—the maximum displacement from equilibrium

hints.rationale: A is the amplitude, the largest magnitude x reaches.

hints.prompt: The position x oscillates (so it isn't a single value 0) and cannot range as '−t to +t' (that would be a time, not a position). Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: x is not fixed at 0 and cannot range over a time '−t to +t', so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: The non-conservative damping force removes energy from a system in which form?
questions.explanation: Damping (such as friction or air resistance) converts the oscillator's mechanical energy into thermal energy (heat), which is dissipated to the surroundings.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (damping dissipates energy as heat).

hints.prompt: Non-conservative damping forces (friction, air resistance) convert mechanical energy into:
hints.rationale: Damping dissipates mechanical energy as heat.

hints.prompt: A student says the mechanical energy removed by damping is simply STORED and can be fully recovered. Is that correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—it is dissipated to the surroundings and not recoverable as mechanical energy

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—it is stored as potential energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—it is stored as kinetic energy

hints.rationale: Damping dissipates energy irreversibly; it is not merely stored.

hints.prompt: Damping definitely removes energy (so 'none' is wrong), and a simple mechanical oscillator does not generate electrical energy. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: No electrical energy is produced and energy is definitely removed, so 'electrical' and 'none' are eliminated.

questions.stem: The time rate of change of mechanical energy for a damped oscillator is always:
questions.explanation: A damping force continually removes mechanical energy from the system, so the mechanical energy decreases over time; its time rate of change is always negative (until motion stops).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (damping monotonically reduces mechanical energy).

hints.prompt: A damping force continuously ____ the mechanical energy of an oscillator over time.
hints.rationale: Damping steadily removes mechanical energy.

hints.prompt: While the damped oscillator is still moving, is there any moment when its mechanical energy stops changing (rate = 0)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—while damping acts and it moves, energy is continually being lost

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—the rate is positive at times

hints.rationale: As long as damping acts during motion, energy is continually removed, so the rate is never zero or positive.

hints.prompt: Damping removes energy, so the rate of change is neither positive (energy isn't gained) nor undefined (it is a well-defined rate). Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Energy is not gained (not positive) and the rate is well-defined (not undefined), so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: A transverse mechanical wave is traveling left to right. Which of the following is correct about the motion of the particles in the wave?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The particles move up and down when the wave travels in a vacuum.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The particles move left and right when the wave travels in a medium.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The particles move up and down when the wave travels in a medium.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The particles move right and left when the wave travels in a vacuum.

questions.explanation: In a transverse wave the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of propagation (up and down for a wave moving horizontally). A mechanical wave also requires a medium, so the correct statement specifies motion in a medium.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (transverse = perpendicular particle motion; mechanical wave needs a medium).

hints.prompt: In a TRANSVERSE wave, the particles of the medium oscillate:

choices.label: A
choices.text: perpendicular to the direction the wave travels

choices.label: B
choices.text: parallel to the direction the wave travels

hints.rationale: Transverse means particle motion is perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

hints.prompt: A MECHANICAL wave (like a wave on a rope) requires:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a material medium to travel through

hints.rationale: Mechanical waves need a medium and cannot travel through a vacuum.

hints.prompt: A mechanical wave cannot travel in a vacuum. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'particles move up and down when the wave travels in a vacuum' and 'particles move right and left when the wave travels in a vacuum'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'particles move left and right when the wave travels in a medium' and 'particles move up and down when the wave travels in a medium'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'particles move up and down when the wave travels in a vacuum' and 'particles move up and down when the wave travels in a medium'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'particles move right and left when the wave travels in a vacuum' and 'particles move left and right when the wave travels in a medium'

hints.rationale: Mechanical waves need a medium, so both 'in a vacuum' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: A guitar string has a number of frequencies at which it vibrates naturally. Which of the following is true in this context?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The resonant frequencies of the string are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The resonant frequencies of the string are not integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The resonant frequencies are unrelated to the fundamental frequency.

questions.explanation: A string fixed at both ends supports standing waves whose resonant (natural) frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental: f_n = n·f₁. These higher harmonics are the overtones.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + options scraped from OpenStax (one redundant 'harmonic overtones' distractor replaced with a clearer unrelated-frequencies distractor to keep a single best answer). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (f_n = n·f₁ for a string fixed at both ends).

hints.prompt: A string fixed at both ends supports standing waves whose natural (resonant) frequencies are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: integer multiples of the fundamental frequency (f, 2f, 3f, ...)

hints.rationale: A string fixed at both ends resonates at f_n = n·f₁.

hints.prompt: These integer-multiple resonant frequencies above the fundamental are collectively called:
hints.rationale: The higher resonant frequencies are the harmonics (overtones).

hints.prompt: The resonant frequencies of a fixed string are definitely related to its fundamental. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'The resonant frequencies are unrelated to the fundamental frequency.' and 'None of the above'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The resonant frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.' and 'The resonant frequencies are not integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'The resonant frequencies are unrelated to the fundamental frequency.' and 'The resonant frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'None of the above' and 'The resonant frequencies are not integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.'

hints.rationale: The frequencies are related to the fundamental, so 'unrelated' and 'none of the above' are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two sine waves travel in the same direction in a medium. The amplitude of each wave is A, and the phase difference between the two is 180°. What is the resultant amplitude?
questions.explanation: A 180° phase difference means the two equal-amplitude waves are exactly out of phase, so their crests align with troughs. They interfere completely destructively, giving a resultant amplitude of A - A = 0.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 16 (Oscillatory Motion and Waves) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (180° out of phase ⇒ destructive interference).

hints.prompt: Two waves that are 180° out of phase (a crest aligns with a trough) interfere:

choices.label: D
choices.text: only if they have different amplitudes

hints.rationale: A 180° phase difference gives destructive interference.

hints.prompt: How does 180°-out-of-phase (destructive) interference differ from IN-phase (constructive) interference for two equal waves?

choices.label: A
choices.text: destructive makes the amplitudes subtract, while constructive makes them add

choices.label: D
choices.text: interference has no effect on amplitude

hints.rationale: Out of phase, amplitudes subtract; in phase, they add.

hints.prompt: Destructive interference makes the resultant SMALLER, so amplitudes larger than a single wave (like 3A or 9A) are impossible. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Destructive interference can't increase the amplitude, so 3A and 9A are impossible and eliminated.

questions.stem: A baggage handler stands on the edge of a runway as a landing plane approaches. Compared to the pitch of the plane's sound as heard by the plane's pilot, which of the following correctly describes the sensation experienced by the handler?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The frequency of the plane will be lower pitched according to the baggage handler and will become even lower pitched as the plane slows to a stop.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The frequency of the plane will be lower pitched according to the baggage handler but will increase in pitch as the plane slows to a stop.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The frequency of the plane will be higher pitched according to the baggage handler but will decrease in pitch as the plane slows to a stop.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The frequency of the plane will be higher pitched according to the baggage handler and will further increase in pitch as the plane slows to a stop.

questions.explanation: Because the source is approaching the handler, the Doppler effect shifts the perceived frequency upward (higher pitch than the pilot hears). As the plane decelerates, the approach speed decreases, so the Doppler shift shrinks and the perceived pitch drops back toward the true frequency.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 17 (Physics of Hearing) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the Doppler effect for an approaching source.

hints.prompt: By the Doppler effect, sound from a source moving TOWARD you is perceived at a:
hints.rationale: An approaching source raises the perceived pitch.

hints.prompt: As the approaching plane DECELERATES (its approach speed decreases), the amount of Doppler shift:

choices.label: A
choices.text: shrinks, so the perceived pitch drops back toward the true frequency

choices.label: B
choices.text: grows, so the pitch rises further

choices.label: D
choices.text: reverses to below the true frequency

hints.rationale: A smaller approach speed means a smaller upward shift, so the pitch falls toward the true value.

hints.prompt: Because the plane is APPROACHING, the handler hears a HIGHER pitch than the pilot, so any 'lower pitched' option is wrong. Which pair can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'lower pitched..., even lower as it slows' and 'lower pitched..., increases in pitch as it slows'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'higher pitched..., decreases in pitch as it slows' and 'higher pitched..., further increases as it slows'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'lower pitched..., even lower as it slows' and 'higher pitched..., decreases in pitch as it slows'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'lower pitched..., increases as it slows' and 'higher pitched..., further increases as it slows'

hints.rationale: An approaching source raises the pitch, so both 'lower pitched' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: A tube is open at one end (and closed at the other). If the fundamental frequency f is created by a wavelength λ, which of the following describes the frequency and wavelength associated with the tube's fourth overtone?
questions.explanation: A tube closed at one end supports only odd harmonics: f, 3f, 5f, 7f, 9f, ... The fundamental is the 1st harmonic, so the overtones are the 3rd, 5th, 7th, and 9th harmonics. The fourth overtone is therefore the 9th harmonic, with frequency 9f and wavelength λ/9.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 17 (Physics of Hearing) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Reformatted from the source's 4-row answer table into A-D choices without changing wording. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (closed pipe: only odd harmonics; 4th overtone = 9th harmonic).

hints.prompt: A tube closed at one end (open at the other) supports standing waves at:
hints.rationale: A closed-end pipe resonates only at odd harmonics.

hints.prompt: Listing the odd harmonics f, 3f, 5f, 7f, 9f..., the fundamental is the 1st, so the overtones are the 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th. Which harmonic is the FOURTH overtone?
hints.rationale: The overtones after the fundamental are 3f, 5f, 7f, 9f, so the fourth overtone is the 9th harmonic.

hints.prompt: A closed-end pipe has only ODD harmonics, so a frequency of 4f is impossible. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 4f is an even harmonic, impossible for a closed pipe, so both '4f' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: A string is attached to an oscillator at one end, passed over a pulley, and attached to a suspended weight at the other end. A standing wave with one antinode forms on the horizontal segment when the oscillator frequency is f₀. The oscillator is then moved to shorten the horizontal segment of string to half its original length. At what frequency will a standing wave with one antinode now form?

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no frequency at which a standing wave will be formed.

questions.explanation: The tension (set by the hanging weight) and the string's linear density are unchanged, so the wave speed v is unchanged. The fundamental (one-antinode) frequency is f = v/(2L). Halving L doubles the frequency: 2f₀.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 17 (Physics of Hearing) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim (figure description summarized in words). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (v fixed by tension; f = v/2L, so halving L doubles f).

hints.prompt: The fundamental (one-antinode) frequency of a vibrating string is f = v/(2L), where the wave speed v depends on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the tension and the string's linear density (mass per length)

hints.rationale: Wave speed v is set by tension and linear density, not by length.

hints.prompt: When the oscillator is moved to shorten the string, which quantity stays the SAME (letting you relate the new frequency to the old)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the wave speed v (tension and linear density are unchanged)

choices.label: D
choices.text: the number of antinodes times the length

hints.rationale: Tension and linear density are unchanged, so v stays the same while L changes.

hints.prompt: A SHORTER string (same wave speed) has a HIGHER fundamental frequency, so a lower or unchanged frequency is wrong. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: B
choices.text: '2f₀' and 'There is no frequency at which a standing wave will be formed.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'f₀' and 'There is no frequency at which a standing wave will be formed.'

hints.rationale: Shortening the string raises the fundamental frequency, so f₀/2 (lower) and f₀ (unchanged) are eliminated.
